title: Biosphere-Atmosphere Chemical Interaction (BACI) Research Group
text:
Welcome
We study exchanges of reactive trace gases between ecosystems and atmosphere using lab experiments, field measurements, and numerical models at Nanjing-Helsinki Institute in Atmospheric and Earth System Sciences, Nanjing University, Suzhou, China.

title: Biosphere-Atmosphere Chemical Interaction (BACI) Research Group
text:
Research areas

Biogenic Volatile Organic Compounds (BVOCs)
Ecological sources and sinks of reactive gases
Extreme events and ecosystem stress
Urban greening and air quality
Earth system modeling

title: Overview
text: Ecosystems release and take up reactive gases, including volatile organic compounds (VOCs), reactive nitrogen species, sulfur compounds, and halogens, linking biological processes from leaves to ecosystems and the atmosphere. Our research combines laboratory experiments, field observations, satellite data, numerical modeling, and data analysis to understand biosphere–atmosphere exchange across scales from seconds to decades and from leaves to continents. By connecting physiological measurements, canopy–ecosystem models, and Earth system frameworks, we aim to reveal the mechanisms controlling reactive trace gas exchange, understand how ecosystem-level complexity emerges in the real world, and assess its broader consequences for atmospheric chemistry, air quality, and climate.

title: Overview
section: BVOC measurements in the laboratory environment
text:
BVOC measurements in the laboratory environment
We use leaf chambers to understand the physiological responses of BVOC emissions to different controlling factors, including light, temperature, and environmental stresses, which form the basis for understanding the behavior of vegetation in response to global environmental change.






Custom-built leaf chamber







(a, b) Comparison of isoprene temperature response curves across different studies. (c) The inverse relationship between emission factors and temperature sensitivity in Arctic sedges.





Reference

Wang, H., Welch, A. M., Nagalingam, S., Leong, C., Czimczik, C. I., Tang, J., Seco, R., Rinnan, R., Vettikkat, L., Schobesberger, S., et al. High temperature sensitivity of Arctic isoprene emissions explained by sedges. Nature Communications, 15(1), 6144.
Wang, H., Welch, A., Nagalingam, S., Leong, C., Kittitananuvong, P., Barsanti, K. C., Sheesley, R. J., Czimczik, C. I., and Guenther, A. B. Arctic heatwaves could significantly influence the isoprene emissions from shrubs. Geophysical Research Letters, 51(2), e2023GL107599.

title: Overview
text:
Environmental change and air quality
Rapid environmental changes (e.g., climate change, land-use change, and anthropogenic emissions) could reshape urban air quality. We aim to understand the role of the biosphere in this process, specifically how biospheric changes and responses affect both ozone and particles.






Conceptual figure showing how heatwaves affect ozone pollution through biospheric responses.







Mobile lab measurements in Los Angeles revealed widespread anthropogenic monoterpene emissions from the food industry and small businesses.





Reference

Wang, H., Nagalingam, S., Welch, A. M., Leong, C., Czimczik, C. I., and Guenther, A. B. Heat waves may trigger unexpected surge in aerosol and ozone precursor emissions from sedges in urban landscapes. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 121(45), e2412817121.
Zhang, Y., Wu, K., Wang, H.*, Pfannerstill, E. Y., Nagalingam, S., Coggon, M. M., Stockwell, C., Ruiz, M. V., Ho, C., Yu, Y., et al. Reconciling observed and modeled estimates of urban terpenoid emissions. (Accepted by Nature Communications, corresponding author)

title: Overview
text:
BVOC emissions and climate
Changes in BVOC emissions can disturb tropospheric chemistry and affect the concentrations of OH, ozone, and aerosols, which in turn influences climate. We aim to elucidate the role of BVOCs in the climate system by integrating field measurements with climate modeling via the Community Earth System Model (CESM).




Herbivory-induced changes in BVOC emissions across Scandinavia could alter cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) concentrations and subsequently disturb weather systems.



Reference

Tang, J., Wang, H.*, Li, T., Cai, Z., Guenther, A., Olsson, P.-O., Schurgers, G., Rieksta, J., He, C., Tang, W., et al. Moth Effect: Herbivore-induced plant volatiles disturb weather patterns. （Under review in Science Advances, co-first and corresponding author）

Modeling isoprene emission response to drought and heatwaves within MEGAN using evapotranspiration data and by coupling with the community land model. Journal of Advances in Modeling Earth Systems, 14(12), e2022MS003174.
Wang, H., Wu, Q., Guenther, A. B., Yang, X., Wang, L., Xiao, T., Li, J., Feng, J., Xu, Q., and Cheng, H. A long-term estimation of biogenic volatile organic compound (BVOC) emission in China from 2001–2016: The roles of land cover change and climate variability. Atmospheric Chemistry and Physics, 21(6), 4825–4848.

title: Projects
text: This project examines the carbon removal potential, ecological risks, and recovery timescales of ocean iron fertilization using Earth system model simulations.

title: Projects
text: This project examines the carbon removal potential, ecological risks, and recovery timescales of ocean iron fertilization using Earth system model simulations.

title: Projects
text:
Marine ecosystem modeling
This project studies phytoplankton and zooplankton dynamics using multiple plankton functional types.

title: Projects
section: Wildfire smoke and urban air quality
text:
Wildfire smoke and urban air quality
This project studies how wildfire smoke interacts with urban pollution and temperature to affect ozone formation.

title: Projects
text:
Data-driven Earth system analysis
This project develops workflows for analyzing large model outputs, including NetCDF files, ensemble simulations, and statistical diagnostics.
